source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
title: Ensure access keys are rotated every 90 days or less

input_row.source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure access keys are rotated every 90 days or less
input_row.description: Access keys consist of an access key ID and secret access key, which are used to sign programmatic requests that you make to AWS. AWS users need their own access keys to make programmatic calls to AWS from the AWS Command Line Interface (AWS CLI), Tools for Windows PowerShell, the AWS SDKs, or direct HTTP calls using the APIs for individual AWS services. It is recommended that all access keys be rotated regularly.
input_row.rationale: Rotating access keys will reduce the window of opportunity for an access key that is associated with a compromised or terminated account to be used. Access keys should be rotated to ensure that data cannot be accessed with an old key which might have been lost, cracked, or stolen.
input_row.audit: Perform the following to determine if access keys are rotated as prescribed: From Console: 1. Go to the Management Console (https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam) 2. Click on Users 3. For each user, go to Security Credentials 4. Review each key under Access Keys 5. For each key that shows Active for status, ensure that Created is less than or equal to 90 days ago. From Command Line: aws iam generate-credential-report aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d The access_key_1_last_rotated and the access_key_2_last_rotated fields in this file notes the date and time, in ISO 8601 date-time format, when the user's access key was created or last changed. If the user does not have an active access key, the value in this field is N/A (not applicable).
input_row.remediation: Perform the following to rotate access keys: From Console: 1. Go to the Management Console (https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam) 2. Click on Users 3. Click on Security Credentials 4. As an Administrator o Click on Make Inactive for keys that have not been rotated in 90 Days 5. As an IAM User o Click on Make Inactive or Delete for keys which have not been rotated or used in 90 Days 6. Click on Create Access Key 7. Update programmatic calls with new Access Key credentials From Command Line: 1. While the first access key is still active, create a second access key, which is active by default. Run the following command: aws iam create-access-key At this point, the user has two active access keys. 2. Update all applications and tools to use the new access key. 3. Determine whether the first access key is still in use by using this command: aws iam get-access-key-last-used 4. One approach is to wait several days and then check the old access key for any use before proceeding. Even if step 3 indicates no use of the old key, it is recommended that you do not immediately delete the first access key. Instead, change the state of the first access key to Inactive using this command: aws iam update-access-key 5. Use only the new access key to confirm that your applications are working. Any applications and tools that still use the original access key will stop working at this point because they no longer have access to AWS resources. If you find such an application or tool, you can switch its state back to Active to reenable the first access key. Then return to step 2 and update this application to use the new key. 6. After you wait some period of time to ensure that all applications and tools have been updated, you can delete the first access key with this command: aws iam delete-access-key References: 1. CCE-78902-4 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/best-practices.html#rotate- credentials 3. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_finding- unused.html 4. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/managing-aws-access-keys.html 5. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_access- keys.html

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control "Ensure access keys are rotated every 90 days or less" pertains to managing IAM credentials within AWS. This is a standard property of the AWS IAM service and can be queried through AWS APIs. The audit information provided includes commands and processes that can be fully automated using AWS CLI or the boto3 SDK, which can check the rotation date of access keys by generating and analyzing a credential report. Therefore, this control is best suited for an Automated audit approach.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_iam_access_key_rotation_within_90_days

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **AWS API Call:** Use the `boto3` SDK's IAM client to generate and retrieve the AWS IAM credential report.
  - `generate_credential_report()` to generate the report.
  - `get_credential_report()` to retrieve the generated report.
- **AWS CLI Command:** Alternatively, use the AWS CLI commands:
  - `aws iam generate-credential-report`
  - `aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d`
- **Specific Resources to Enumerate:** Enumerate all IAM users within the AWS account.
- **Configuration Properties to Validate:**
  - Parse the credential report to check the `access_key_1_last_rotated` and `access_key_2_last_rotated` fields.
  - Ensure these fields indicate a rotation or creation date within the last 90 days for any active access keys.
  - If the field value is `N/A`, it implies no active keys, which is compliant.
- **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
  - **Pass:** All active access keys have been rotated within 90 days.
  - **Fail:** Any active access key has not been rotated within the last 90 days.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of this control as suitable for an Automated audit approach is correct. AWS provides APIs and CLI commands that allow for the generation and retrieval of IAM credential reports, making it feasible to automate the checking of access key rotation dates.
- The use of AWS APIs (`boto3` SDK) and AWS CLI commands is appropriate for this control, as they can effectively retrieve the necessary data to verify compliance.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9

- The automation details provided are technically accurate. The use of `generate_credential_report()` and `get_credential_report()` methods in `boto3` is correct for generating and retrieving the IAM credential report.
- The AWS CLI commands `aws iam generate-credential-report` and `aws iam get-credential-report` are valid and correctly described.
- The program name `aws_iam_access_key_rotation_within_90_days` is descriptive and follows the naming convention well, indicating the specific check being performed.
- The only minor improvement could be ensuring that the decoding of the base64 content in the CLI command is clearly explained, as this is necessary for parsing the report.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Program Name:** While the current name is descriptive, it could be slightly improved for clarity by including the term "check" to indicate the action, such as `aws_iam_check_access_key_rotation_within_90_days`.
- **Consideration:** It would be beneficial to mention handling potential errors when generating or retrieving the credential report, such as handling exceptions in `boto3` or checking for errors in CLI command execution.
- **Alternative Methods:** Consider using AWS Config to monitor access key rotation as an additional layer of compliance checking, although this would be more of a supplementary measure rather than a replacement for the current approach.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded and aligns with AWS capabilities for automating the audit of IAM access key rotation.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Keep as-is

- The current assessment is accurate and well-structured for automated compliance checking. The suggested improvements are minor and do not necessitate changes to the core approach.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that AWS exposes all necessary data for this control via the IAM credential report, accessible through both the AWS CLI and boto3 API. The audit steps outlined in the control map directly to these programmatic capabilities, allowing for a fully automated check of access key rotation dates for all IAM users. No manual inspection or console-only data is required.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
aws_iam_access_key_rotation_within_90_days

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **AWS Resource/Service:** IAM Users (Access Keys)
- **API/CLI Method:**  
  - boto3: `iam.generate_credential_report()`, `iam.get_credential_report()`
  - AWS CLI: `aws iam generate-credential-report`, `aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d`
- **Properties to Check:**  
  - For each IAM user, examine `access_key_1_active`, `access_key_1_last_rotated`, `access_key_2_active`, and `access_key_2_last_rotated` fields in the credential report.
- **Pass Criteria:**  
  - All active access keys (`*_active` = TRUE) have a `*_last_rotated` date within the last 90 days.
  - If a user has no active access keys, or the fields are `N/A`, this is compliant.
- **Fail Criteria:**  
  - Any active access key with a `*_last_rotated` date older than 90 days, or missing rotation data for an active key.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
There were no substantive disagreements between the AI opinions; both supported automation. The only minor difference was a suggestion to refine the program name, but the original (`aws_iam_access_key_rotation_within_90_days`) is sufficiently descriptive and aligns with AWS naming conventions.

**SOURCE:** CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
